chapter_context.summary: Warns against going down to Egypt for horses and trusting in chariots, declaring that the Egyptians are men and not God, and promising the Lord's defense of Jerusalem.

outline.text: Woe to Those Relying on Chariots and Egypt's Chivalry
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Woe to Those Relying on Chariots and Egypt's Chivalry (31:1-3), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Lord Defending Jerusalem and Ruin of Assyria's Young Men
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Lord Defending Jerusalem and Ruin of Assyria's Young Men (31:4-9), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

key_themes.title: Prophecy of Judgment
key_themes.description: This chapter explores key facets of prophecy of judgment, revealing how God's holy character directs both judgment and redemption.

key_themes.title: The Holy One of Israel
key_themes.description: Isaiah's signature title for God, highlighting His absolute transcendent holiness, moral purity, and unique relationship with His people.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the warnings and promises of Chapter 31, how does God's double character of absolute holiness and deep comfort speak to your life today?
How does this chapter encourage you to trust in God's promises of ultimate renewal and restoration in times of spiritual or physical exile?

cultural_historical.title: The Role of the Prophet in Israel
cultural_historical.text: Prophets in the Hebrew Bible were not just fortune-tellers, but 'covenant enforcement agents'—called to confront sin, demand social justice, and bring hope of future restoration.
